data.title: Rates Spark: No-Brain Steepeners Exposed To Reversals
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Michiel Tukker, Benjamin Schroeder

US yields find an excuse to ratchet a tad lower, against the grain of the crowded steeper

Thursday saw firmer US data than anticipated. Second-quarter GDP was revised up a smidgen to 3.3% (from 3.1%). These data have been distorted by net import gyrations on the back of the tariff games being played by importers. The same swings can be seen in inventories. Consumer spending was quite tame though, at 1.6%, which is a better measure of where the economy really is (technically a growth recession). Jobless claims came in at 229k, and the previous week was revised down. The same for continuing claims. Stop the music here and these claims numbers are actually a sign of a solid labour market.

Yields across the board were a tad up on the back of this. However, it seems the market is primed for steepeners and long-end short positioning. As the day progressed, the path of least resistance was to push against that, and ultimately, long-end rates managed to ease off highs. Month-end duration extensions have been a catalyst, but the price action so far has tended to go beyond simply that. But can also mean some more of the same on Friday. That said, we stick with the steepening view from both ends as our preferred stance in the coming weeks.

We also had a reasonably decent 7yr auction. It tailed slightly (auction yield came slightly above secondary), but that was into a firm market, which raises the tendency to tail. Dealers took down less than 10%, which is good. The indirect bid (includes central banks) dominated, reversing the trend seen in the 2yr and 5yr auctions where the direct bid (where most domestics come in) was firmer than usual. All in all, safely away. The next big impulse comes from Friday's PCE inflation readings.

Euro rates risk getting carried away by falling inflation

In the eurozone, while price pressures seem well-behaved, we identify inflation undershooting as a key tail risk to our rates outlook. Friday’s inflation numbers are unlikely to swing markets, but a series of lower-than-expected figures could reverse some of the recent upward pressure on rates. The short end of the curve is especially vulnerable to a shift lower as markets are now pricing in just 18bp of European Central Bank easing by mid-2026, which is not even a full cut. Having said that, inflation fixtures already account for a period of inflation well below the 2% target.

As fiscal impulses start ramping up, the medium- to longer-term inflation outlook should remain firm, keeping upward pressure on the back end of the curve. This also seems the consensus from the ECB's June meeting minutes, although the inflation outlook is a topic of debate. In our baseline, we think the curve should turn towards hiking mode by the end of 2026 or beginning of 2027 as inflation reverts above target again. As a result, the 10Y swap rate should find itself moving higher from here, steepening the curve.

The question is whether markets can maintain a bearish view on longer rates against a backdrop of falling inflation numbers. European investors are still traumatised by the secular stagnation environment from pre-Covid, one in which inflation was well below target. As such, markets may extrapolate downside inflation surprises too far out in the future, which would then pull down the entire curve. Again, this is not our baseline but is a tail risk we should be aware of.

Friday's events and market view

Germany, France and Spain will release preliminary inflation data for August where the market is looking for slightly higher year-on-year readings compared to last month. Economic sentiment indicators for the eurozone will be watched as to whether they will confirm the somewhat more upbeat take on the economy presented by the recent PMIs. The ECB will publish the consumer inflation expectations survey for July.

In the US, the focus will fall on the PCE price index, the Fed’s favoured inflation measure. The year-on-year rises are expected to come in at an unchanged 2.6% for the headline and a slightly higher 2.9% for the core rate. Our economists think there is a risk for a slightly cooler monthly core reading than the consensus 0.3%: despite very hot producer price figures, the components that feed directly from that release into core PCE were more mixed. Personal income and spending data as well as the final University of Michigan consumer sentiment will round off the US releases. The Fed’s Waller will speak on monetary policy.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: BlackRock New York Municipal Opportunities Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted returns of -0.98% (Institutional shares) and -1.14% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • The fund’s long-term municipal yield curve exposure was the largest detractor as the yield curve twisted (where different segments of the yield curve respond in different, non- parallel ways to each other) and steepened.
  • Duration (sensitivity to interest rate movements) increased, having started the quarter well above the benchmark’s position. The fund retained an overweight exposure to municipal bonds maturing in 15-25 years and an overweight allocation to credit rated A and below.

★★★★★ Morningstar OverallTM

Institutional shares rated against 79 Us Fund Muni New York Long Funds, as of 6/30/25, based on risk-adjusted total return. Ratings are determined monthly and subject to change. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a fund is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics.††

Portfolio management

Sean Carney, Michael Kalinoski, Christian Romaglino, Ryan McDonald, Kevin Maloney

Top 10 holdings (%)

Utility Debt Securitization Au 5 12/15/2040 1.69
Tob 5 44420rbc3 Huygen 144a 1.66
California Infrastructure & Ec 9.5 01/01/2065 1.63
New York Transn Dev Corp Spl F 6 06/30/2054 1.61
New York St Dorm Auth Revs Non 5.5 07/01/2054 1.48
Triborough Brdg & Tunl Auth N 5.25 05/15/2052 1.47
New York St Dorm Auth Revs Non 4.802 12/01/2034 1.43
New York St Dorm Auth Sales Ta 5 03/15/2049 1.42
New York N Y City Transitional 5 05/01/2040 1.42
New York Transn Dev Corp Spl F 5.5 06/30/2060 1.37

Investment approach

*Invests in New York municipal bonds through a flexible investment approach seeking to manage interest rate risk and credit risk while provide a compelling combination of attractive tax-advantaged income, returns and meaningful downside protection.

Contributors

Detractors
Positions in municipal bonds with maturities of about 10 years or less contributed to returns, as those parts of the yield curve performed better. The cash holding was also beneficial.

Allocations to municipal bonds at the long end of the yield curve were the largest detractors as long-term rates rose. The municipal yield curve twisted and steepened, with shorter-maturity areas of the yield curve rallying while the longer end sold off.

Credit risk refers to the possibility that the bond issuer will not be able to make principal and interest payments. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. There may be less information on the financial condition of municipal issuers than public corporations. The market for municipal bonds may be less liquid than taxable bonds. Some investors may be subject to Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT). Capital gains distributions are taxable. BlackRock provides compensation in connection with obtaining or using third-party ratings and rankings.

1 The BBG Municipal Bond Index measures the U.S. municipal market performance of bonds issued by states or municipalities. All bonds in the index are exempt from U.S. federal income taxes or subject to the alternative minimum tax. 2 The BBG Custom New York Municipal Bond Index measures the performance of municipal bonds issued in New York and are exempt from federal income and alternative minimum tax. The state level municipal bond indices consist of bonds that have been issued by municipalities or municipal authorities within the 50 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico and the US Virgin Islands.†† The Morningstar RatingTM for funds, or "star rating", is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open- ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its three-, five-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% three-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% five-year rating/40% three-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% five-year rating/20% three-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent three-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund was rated against the following numbers of U.S.-domiciled Us Fund Muni New York Long funds over the following time periods:79 in the last 3 years, 76 in the last 5 years and 61 in the last 10 years. With respect to these Us Fund Muni New York Long funds, the fund received a Morningstar Rating of 5, 5 and 4 stars for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Other classes may have different performance characteristics.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the fund carefully before investing. data.title: BlackRock Low Duration Bond Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted returns of 1.83% (Institutional shares) and 1.65% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • The fund outperformed its benchmark, led by high yield and investment grade credit, duration (interest rate sensitivity) and yield-curve positioning, agency mortgages, asset- backed securities, commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS), collateralized loan obligations, and non-agency residential mortgages.
  • The fund maintained an overweight duration position. Allocations to investment grade and high yield credit were increased to benefit from spread decompression and compelling fundamentals. We took profits in callable U.S. agency debt.

Portfolio management

Akiva Dickstein, Scott Maclellan, Amanda Liu, Sam Summers, Siddharth Mehta

Top 10 holdings(%)

United States Treasury 5.84
Goldman Sachs Group Inc/The 3.24
JPMorgan Chase 3.07
Fnma_25-44b-Fb 3.04
Fnma_25-40g-Gf 2.29
Morgan Stanley 2.24
Bank of America 2.09
Citigroup 2.08
Fhr_5548-Fc 1.95
HCA 1.66

Investment approach

Invests primarily in investment grade bonds and maintains an average portfolio duration that is between 0-3 years.

Contributors

Detractors

High yield credit was the largest contributor to relative performance. Overweight positions in investment grade credit, duration, agency mortgage- backed securities, and CMBS were also beneficial, as rates rallied and credit spreads tightened. The fund continued to increase its exposures to high yield and investment grade credit to benefit from spread widening, focusing on the technology and financials sectors. We further reduced the allocation to agency callable debt, while we increased exposure to securitized credit to benefit from the new issue calendar and spread decompression.

Underweight positions in U.S. Treasuries, sovereign, supranational, and agency debt, and emerging markets detracted slightly.

Further insight

During the quarter, the fund tactically traded duration with a mild long duration bias. Ongoing tariff uncertainty, fiscal issues, and geopolitical tensions meant it avoided large underweight or overweight duration positions as volatility persisted. Allocations to investment grade and high yield credit were increased to benefit from spread widening following the U.S. tariff announcements. The fund continued to reduce its exposure to agency callable debt, while it increased the allocation to securitized credit to benefit from the new issue calendar and spread decompression.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 08/29/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

data.title: BlackRock High Equity Income Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted returns of 4.41% (Institutional shares) and 4.34% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • The largest contributor to relative performance was security selection in the health care, consumer staples, and consumer discretionary sectors. The largest detractor was investment decisions in the industrials, financials, and utilities sectors.
  • The largest exposures were in the financials, health care, and industrials sectors. During the quarter, the fund increased its allocations to the financials and communication services sectors, and reduced its exposures to the industrials and health care sectors.

Portfolio management

Tony DeSpirito, Kyle McClements, David Zhao, Tom Pierce, Christopher M. Accettella

Top 10 holdings(%)

Amazon (AMZN) 2.48
Citigroup (C) 2.42
Baxter International (BAX) 1.98
Comcast Corp Class A (CMCSA) 1.89
Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) 1.83
First Citizens Bancshares Inc Clas (FCNCA) 1.79
Samsung Electronics Gds Repre- sent (OTCPK:SSNLF) 1.68
Wells Fargo (WFC) 1.54
Wells Fargo-Eln 1.35
Ss And C Technologies Holdings Inc (SSNC) 1.35

Investment Approach

Invests in common stocks that pay dividends and have the potential for capital appreciation. The fund also utilizes an option writing (selling) strategy to enhance distributions paid to the fund's shareholders. The fund invests, under normal market conditions, at least 80% of its total assets in dividend paying equities.

Contributors

Detractors

Stock selection in the health care sector was the largest contributor, specifically in the health care providers & services industry. Security selection in the consumer staples sector, particularly in the consumer staples distribution & retail industry, was beneficial. Lastly, selection decisions in the consumer discretionary sector, specifically in the textiles, apparel & luxury goods industry, was helpful.

The largest detractor was security selection in the industrials sector, most notably in the electrical equipment industry. In the financials sector, an underweight position and selection decisions in the capital markets industry adversely impacted relative performance. Lastly, stock selection in the utilities sector, namely in the electric utilities industry, was a hindrance.

Further insight

At quarter-end, markets remain volatile, shaped by trade and geopolitical tensions, and mixed economic signals. However, optimism about artificial intelligence endures, with companies emphasizing the technology in earnings calls and delivering strong results. We remain anchored in our bottom-up, fundamentals-driven approach. While corporate earnings are healthy, full valuations demand precision and discipline. In this environment, our value-oriented strategy, focused on companies with strong balance sheets, durable cash flows, and reasonable valuations, offers stability. We believe this approach provides critical support, helping to navigate uncertainty and capitalize on opportunities through active management.

data.title: BlackRock Managed Volatility V.I. Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted a return of 2.69% (Class I shares) for the second quarter of 2025, while its benchmark, the BofA Merrill Lynch 3-mo. T-bill Index, returned 1.04%.
  • The fund’s outperformance of its benchmark was led by relative value positions across bonds and currencies, as well as a directional long position in global equities.
  • The fund had a long position in global equities and directionally short duration (interest rate sensitivity) exposures to the United States, Japan, and Germany. It had a short U.S. dollar position versus a basket of developed market currencies. On the relative value side, it had a long position in U.K. gilts versus a short exposure to German bunds for a majority of the quarter.

Portfolio management

Philip Green, Michael Pensky

Investment approach

The fund seeks to attain the highest total investment return through a fully managed investment policy utilizing equity, debt (including money market) and convertible securities.

Contributors

Detractors

Relative value fixed income positions delivered most of the positive performance, helped by a directional long position in global equities. Directional short positions in long-dated U.S. and German duration were also modestly additive, as yield curves steepened globally. Currency positioning added to returns, driven primarily by long yen versus short U.S. dollar positioning as the U.S. currency continued to weaken. Relative value equity industry positions also added to performance, driven by allocations to the health care services and aerospace & defense sectors versus the pharmaceuticals sector.

 Relative value positions in emerging market equities were negative, particularly long positions in Taiwan and Thailand versus Korean equities. Tariff concerns drove weak performance in some Asia-Pacific equity markets, although Korean stocks fared well.

Further insight

We view global recession risks as overpriced and remain pro-cyclically positioned, with long equity and short duration exposures. German fiscal plans and greater clarity on the U.S. administration’s One Big Beautiful Bill point to a broader fiscal impulse. Inflation is likely to remain above target due to strong demand and trade frictions. Additionally, the U.S. administration’s trade policies will likely reduce flows into U.S. assets, exerting downward pressure on the U.S. dollar. The fund has a short position in the U.S. dollar versus other developed markets as well as a short position in long-dated Treasury bonds. We have added to outright and relative value long positions in Japanese equities versus other developed markets.

data.title: BlackRock U.S. Mortgage Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted returns of 1.63% (Institutional shares) and 1.57% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • Allocations to non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) drove performance across a variety of subsectors. The fund’s duration (interest rate sensitivity) and yield-curve positioning was additive, while allocations to agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) were a modest detractor.
  • During the quarter, allocations to asset-backed securities were increased, primarily in consumer-focused collateral, such as home equity loans. Non-agency RMBS holdings were also raised, primarily in AAA rated non-qualified assets. Positions in agency MBS and agency collateralized mortgage obligations were reduced.

Portfolio management

Matthew Kraeger, Siddharth Mehta, Ibrahim Incoglu, Samir Lakhani

Top 10 holdings (%)

Umbs 30yr Tba(reg A)

Gnma_21-64v Ih

Gnma_21-58a Iy

0.63

Investment approach

Invests primarily in mortgage-related securities of any maturity, including U.S. government and agency securities, securities issued by U.S. government instrumentalities and U.S. government-sponsored enterprises and other MBS or mortgage-related securities issued by the U.S. government or by private issuers.

Contributors

Detractors

Allocations to non-agency RMBS were the largest driver of the fund’s performance, primarily single-family rentals, non- qualified mortgages, and non- performing/re-performing loans.

Duration and yield-curve positioning was additive, as were holdings in commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS).

Agency MBS holdings were a modest detractor from performance.

Further insight

We continue to favor structured products, with a focus on higher-quality segments of the market that have strong levels of protection. In particular, we continue to prefer the top-of- the-capital structure across CMBS subsectors backed by more cash-flow-sensitive collateral and are biased toward down-in-credit quality for collateral types with more stable cash-flow profiles. Consumer-backed collateral has been a focus within asset-backed markets, favoring home equity loans. In non-agency RMBS, we find non-qualified mortgages compelling due to favorable convexity (the rate at which duration changes in response to interest rate movements) profiles into a move lower in rates and structures with embedded extension risk.

data.title: Adeia: A New Growth Chapter
data.description:
Adeia Inc. (NASDAQ:ADEA) represents an investment opportunity, being well-positioned to capture the trends of the market in which it operates with a possible expansion of its multiples in the future, along with its increase in revenue. With the signing of new contracts, its stock price could be boosted.

The Company

The company is a Research and Development (R&D) and Intellectual Property (IP) licensing company, operating on a business model focused on the creation and monetization of new technologies. The company comes from a division of Xperi Holding Corp. (XPER) in the year 2022, which split into two different companies, Xperi Inc. and Adeia Inc, with different niches. The company at the moment has two main segments, the first being media, in which it licenses a portfolio of patents covering the entertainment experience, including clients like cable and satellite providers, streaming services, and electronics manufacturers such as consoles and Smart TVs, and the second segment is semiconductors, where it develops and licenses technologies that beat against physical challenges like Moore's Law, with a focus on packaging innovation.

Its vast portfolio is what makes it a strong competitor, as in early 2025, it had more than 12,750 patents and pending applications worldwide, representing an increase of +32% since its separation from its holding company. Currently, approximately 85% of its patents are fruits of internal innovations, generated by its own team.

Its products in the media segment are made for improving how consumers consume content, with interactive programming guides and technologies that organize the presentation of different contents on screen. In the semiconductor segment, its products are innovations that allow the creation of more powerful and smaller chips, with techniques for vertical stacking and connection of chips, and liquid cooling technologies on the chip for AI data centers.

Q2 results

The second quarter ended in June, presenting results that were in line with management's expectations, with its revenue reaching $85.7 million, as an example. Compared to the same period of the previous year, its net income almost doubled in an increase of 99%, totaling $16.7 million.

The company also closed licensing agreements, where three are from new clients in key growths, with the most notable agreement made with ST Microelectronics, and two other new agreements in e-commerce, including Warby Parker. Furthermore, ADEA also launched a product for the AI and computing market, a direct-to-chip liquid cooling technology RapidCool, representing not just another product, but a solution to an issue in the AI niche, the self-heating of its processors.

Using as a starting point the seasonality of the sector, with Q4 being the strongest quarter due to the concentration in contract renewals, it is expected that Q1 and Q2 will be weaker. Using as a base the same quarter of the previous year of $86.1 million, with an estimated growth of 2.6%, motivated by the impact of new contracts, as a lower rate would ignore its new commercial advances with contracts, and a higher one would be very aggressive historically, totaling $88 million for Q3. This estimate is within the company's guidance which forecasts an annual revenue between $390 million and $430 million. Therefore, for this to happen, it would need a realistic growth of $128 million in the last quarter, the strongest for its seasonality.

Sector

The company is part of the global intellectual property licensing market, valued in the year 2024 at $194 billion, with growth rates between 7% and 8% until the year 2032, surpassing $310 billion.

This growth is driven by the acceleration of technological trends, leading companies to hire out development, licensing technologies instead of creating them internally, along with the rise of AI and 5G, intensifying this trend and increasing the volume and demand for licensed patents.

This sector has several other sectorial trends that are driving it, such as the significant increase of AI in the media sector and the great proliferation of content, making Adeia's content discovery and recommendation tool a strong point, possessing tools for subscriber retention on streaming platforms. Another important sector in this trend is semiconductors, which at the moment is facing the physical limitations of Moore's Law, with transistors getting smaller and more expensive, using 3D chip stacking to achieve greater efficiency. The company's hybrid bonding technology is also on point to capture this trend, being fundamental for this transition by allowing dense and more efficient interconnections between the stacked chips.

Both these segments are synergistic, as both are moved by the growth of artificial intelligence use, where the need for these AI algorithms increases for content discovery and recommendation, requiring more efficient semiconductor chips, thereby creating a demand for cooling technologies, such as those offered by ADEA.

Competition

The company is in a diversified competitive landscape, including companies exclusively licensing Pure IP, such as InterDigital (IDCC), which has a large portfolio of mobile and video technologies, and Technology and Ecosystem licensing companies, such as Dolby Laboratories (DLB), its most notable competitor, licensing its audio and image technologies. The main difference between ADEA and its competitors is that it has a strong presence in both these media and semiconductor markets.

ADEA trades with a lower EV/EBITDA among its competitors and is similar to that of IDCC, suggesting that the market is underestimating the growth potential in its profits. DLB has a multiple almost 70% higher, being seen as a higher quality company with more predictable cash flows. The P/E also follows a similar premise; however, it is important to note that IDCC is artificially lower because, upon normalizing its EPS, its P/E would be considered higher, while ADEA is the one with a larger discount among the three, with an EPS of only $0.74.

Valuation

Revenue

Its revenue has been showing a decline in the last two years, reflecting the division of Xperi Holding into two companies, impacting the revenue that was part of a larger entity; however, its revenue at the moment already totals $378.67 million, representing a growth of 5.47% in relation to the previous year, ending its consecutive years of declines, starting again the growth trajectory.

Considering the $173.4 million already made in the first semester and the projection of $88 million for Q3, the company would need a very strong Q4 performance of approximately $149 million to reach $410 million for the full year. This figure, which aligns with the midpoint of the company's guidance, is achievable due to the significant seasonality of the business and reflects management's confidence in closing key deals by year-end.

The projection for 2026 is more speculative, depending entirely on the company's growth, developed from the calculation of its annual growth rate. Its legacy PayTV business is still relevant and should continue with an annual decline performance of -1% to -3%, being a brake on its total growth, and a growth in its new verticals, as its e-commerce segment should present strong growth, becoming its main engine. Using as a base $410 million from the previous year, and applying a prudent annual growth rate of 4%, the total revenue for 2026 will reach $426.4 million. This scenario represents a new phase of growth for the company, overcoming its loss of tens of dollars from its Comcast contract, which expired at the end of the year 2022.

EV/EBITDA

In comparison to the market leader, ADEA's EV/EBITDA presents a discount of almost 40%, indicating that perhaps the market does not give it the same credit for the predictability in its cash flow. This multiple is lower as the company is still in transition, needing to prove that its growth in new areas will be enough to compensate for the decline of recent years.

This multiple should be between the range of 12x to 14x, as a multiple below 10x would be conservative, considering the future growth of the company. This multiple does not assume that the company will have growth to the point of becoming equal to Dolby at 17.16x, but that it will be recognized as a company of more visible growth and of lower risk. With this increase in its multiple, its stock price would have an increase of 37% in relation to the current value of $15.36, reaching the value of $21.04, along with its Market Cap reaching over $2 billion.

Drivers and Risks

I believe that new high-value contracts are the main short-term growth engine for the company, as the signing of one or more contracts from large-scale companies and semiconductor manufacturers, or companies with media platforms, would help to bring a significant boost in revenues that have been showing a decline in recent years, but mainly, would help to boost profits. If its new technology is adopted by large companies, not only would it be an addition of recurring revenue with high margin, but it would also bring greater relevance to its media portfolio, and its adoption in data centers or hardware manufacturers by large operators would open a new and high-growth complementary revenue line. With the revenue from its semiconductors and other areas increasing, the market would tend to apply higher multiples to the company, bringing it closer to its competitors.

The company has been in a patent infringement process since the end of 2024, alleging that the Disney defendants infringe nine patents from Adeia's media intellectual property portfolio. The patents at issue relate to various aspects of media streaming and content delivery technology. The victory in this agreement would not only bring the company a huge financial gain but would also be another great validation in its portfolio.

In contrast, its biggest risk at the moment is the irregularity in its revenues, depending entirely on the closing of deals and contracts. If one deal planned for closing is canceled or moved to the next quarter, the company may end up not reaching its annual revenue goal, leading to a negative market reaction, and could even generate declines in its stock. Using Comcast as an example, where the loss of just this single large-scale client can drastically change its annual revenue, indicating that the non-renewal or loss of an important contract can be a big issue.

Conclusion

The company has potential for strong cash generation and higher profit margins, showing to have capacity to become a potential player in the market. As the company's revenue grows, the market will reconsider it with higher multiples, leading to its expansion. With a target price of $21.04, I recommend buying this stock.

data.title: The Gap: Increasingly Attractive With Tariffs Priced In
data.description:
Shares of The Gap (NYSE:GAP) have been a poor performer this past year, losing about 3% of their value, as its turnaround has faced headwinds from weakness in Athleta and pressures from tariffs. Those came to a head on Thursday with the stock plunging over 7% following disappointing results. I last covered shares of The Gap in June, downgrading shares to a “hold” given my concerns around tariffs. Since then, the stock has returned 4%, lagging the market’s 8% gain, consistent with my rating. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit shares. I believe we are nearing a buy point.

In the company’s second quarter, The Gap earned $0.57, which beat estimates by $0.02 as revenue was flat from a year ago, though we are yet to feel the full impact from tariffs. During Q2, 10% tariffs were largely in place as negotiations were underway, and the tariffs on many countries like Vietnam are now higher. Plus, retailers of course carry inventory, which further delays the impact of tariffs on their financials. Earnings were up 6% from last year as its cost-cutting efforts are helping to support margins. As you can see below, there was widespread divergence among brands.

While total revenue was flat, in-store revenue was down slightly. Conversely, online was up 3% and now represents 34% of sales. Gross margins decreased by 140bps to 41.2%, primarily due to savings last year on its credit card. Rent and occupancy were 10 bps better given stronger comparable sales. Overall, the company had an operating margin of 7.8%.

Looking more deeply at segment results, Old Navy same-store sales were up 2% with revenue coming in at $2.2 billion. Despite the company’s name, this unit accounts for the majority of sales and is the most important contributor. It has also provided the steadiest performance throughout the turnaround, and these results were consistent with its slowing but steady growth.

Gap same-store sales were up 4% with revenue up 1% to $772 million, given a lower store count. The namesake brand has regained relevance among consumers with reinvigorated product launches and a new viral ad campaign. I am cautiously optimistic on this brand continuing to exhibit strength.

Banana Republic has had a difficult time turning around, taking much longer than Old Navy and Gap; however, here we are starting to see some progress. Comps were up 4% with sales of $475 million, down 1%, as it has closed underperforming locations. I do believe shrinking the brand and refocusing it is the right move, and it seems the company is making some progress here. That said, given a history of ups and downs, I would like to see several quarters of solid comp growth before believing it has truly turned the corner.

Finally, Athleta was the major underperformer with comparable store sales down 9% from a year ago. This is its smallest unit with $300 million in sales. While athleisure has strong fundamentals, the market has softened since the height of work from home COVID era sales, and Athleta has frankly struggled to build a following beyond its core base consistently and is likely to remain a niche player. I remain unconvinced in a turnaround here and would rather see the company explore a sale of the brand, though I concede that is unlikely.

Inventories were up 9% to $2.3 billion due to “accelerated receipts” and tariff costs. At this time, I am comfortable with inventories outpacing sales as companies sought to front-run tariffs to reduce 2025 costs. I expect inventories to come down sequentially. Still, I am concerned that, like businesses, consumers sought to front-run tariffs, which could weigh on results in the second half of the year.

As I have discussed in the past, apparel retailers faced a multiyear hangover from 2022-2024 as consumer spending slowed after surging to unsustainable levels post-COVID. This initial surge left many carrying too much inventory, which they spent years working down. My view was that by late 2024, the market had returned to balance, and we could see apparel growth run in the 0-2% area this year.

We have actually seen apparel spending exceed this benchmark, rising over 4%. While the fundamentals of the sector are definitely better, I do not expect this level of spending to persist. Given how much publicity tariffs have received and with apparel being particularly tariff sensitive, I suspect many consumers pulled forward discretionary purchases into Q2 to front run tariffs and mitigate potential inflation. As such, I am cautious that we may see a bit of a slowdown in the second half of the year as demand was cannibalized by a strong Q2.

Importantly, Gap maintains a solid balance sheet with $2.4 billion of cash against $1.5 billion of debt and $3.5 billion of operating leases. The company did $82 million of buybacks in the quarter, and I do see scope to accelerate the pace, which could support shares. It also offers a 3% dividend yield, which I view as secure.

Alongside results, management did reaffirm its 1-2% sales growth target for the year. However, it now expects tariffs to cost it $150-$175 million, up from $100-$150 million previously. This is more in keeping with my prior view that potential tariff deals, particularly with Vietnam, were likely to have a larger impact of $200 million annually. At the midpoint of guidance, it now expects about $1.05 billion in operating income from $1.2 billion previously. This decline matches its tariff cost projection and is more reasonable in my view.

Given the weaker outlook, Gap did cut its cap-ex budget by $75 million. This guidance implies just over $700 million in free cash flow this calendar year. The company has generated $127 million of free cash flow year to date given about $350 million of working capital drains. As these partially reverse (as is the seasonal norm), we should see a meaningful acceleration of cash flow.

Back in June, I expected $650 million in go-forward free cash flow. Frankly, I view guidance as consistent with my view on a go-forward basis because calendar 2025 has just a partial tariff impact and I expect, even with mitigation efforts, there to be some incremental pressure on cash flow in 2026 from tariffs. That gives shares ~$1.75 of forward free cash flow. Given modest growth but the potential for a slower second half, I continue to view a ~8% free cash flow yield as fair for shares.

As such, I continue to see fair value of around $22. With their drop after hours, shares are below $21, creating an 8% total return opportunity, including the dividend. While not quite there, I believe we are nearing a buy level given its excellent balance sheet and solid results at its two biggest brands. While I am remaining a “hold” at current levels, I would use a pullback below $20 to begin adding shares as that price more than fully compensates investors for the incremental cost of tariffs.

data.title: Netflix: Growth Priced In, Expect Consolidation
data.description:
Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) is a phenomenal growth story with a bright future outlook — but for now, I think the growth is priced in, and shares are likely to consolidate for a while. The global media and technology company experienced a significant 75% rally over the past 12 months, indicating there may be limited upside ahead.

A massive stock price appreciation was deserved. Top and bottom lines grew in double-digits while securing a dominant stance in the entertainment landscape. The company is not expecting to slow down — in its recent shareholder letter, Netflix raised its guidance to $44.8 billion to $45.2 billion from a previous $43.5 billion to $44.5 billion, a roughly 2.5% increase, suggesting impressive 15% to 16% year-over-year growth.

Nevertheless, I think its valuation has run ahead of its fundamentals, and I see the stock consolidating from here. That is why I think Netflix is a Hold, while I expect the company to trade in the range of $1,100 to $1,300 over the next 12 months, without indicating a strong further direction. It is a high-quality business, but also expensive, with little room for error.

Q2 2025: Growth Is Not Slowing And Valuations Reflect That Loud And Clear

On June 13, the company disclosed its Q2 2025 report and satisfied investors, assuring great overall performance. Netflix reported an impressive quarterly revenue increase of 16% year-over-year to approximately $11.08 billion. The bottom line grew remarkably, about 47%, at $7.19 earnings per share, compared to $4.88 last year. EPS expansion is one of the main reasons why the company trades at such high valuations — at a forward P/E of 46.31, roughly 3x higher than peers and nearly 80% richer than the average S&P500 company, I think a lot of growth is already priced in.

To put it in perspective, if we take into account Wall Street analysts' estimates for the years ahead, with a significant 20% to 30% annual growth in EPS, Netflix would, in about 36 months from now, come down closer to market-average valuations.

This is already a massive bottom-line growth and leaves a small margin for error, indicating that if there's still upside left, risk may be too high to justify taking the chance. I think that the opportunity costs of considering Netflix are also a bit too high. It is a great business, but the stock is simply expensive.

Management disclosed that it expects Netflix's operating margins to come in around 31%, mainly driven by member growth, continued price increases, and ad revenue. Netflix offered multiple high-performing series throughout the quarter, such as Squid Game Season 3 and Ginny & Georgia Season 3, which significantly boosted growth in the member count. Investors should be satisfied with Netflix's production and distribution, which play a major role in the company's success.

Valuations: Despite Massive Growth, I think Upside Is Limited

I have mentioned before that Netflix is an exceptional growth story. It is fascinating that in such a saturated digital media market, it manages to grow at this pace. For instance, Netflix's revenue grew 14.84% year-over-year, over 4x compared to peers' average of 3.28%. Its EBITDA grew over 40%, while the sector median remained around 6%. Netflix is not only a rapid top-line growth story, but also a bottom-line one. Over the past year, its diluted EPS increased by 46.83%, which is roughly 2.3x higher than the industry average.

A significant contribution to the profitability came from the strategic pricing initiatives by raising prices in particular regions. Their ad-supported membership tier proved successful, with expectations that the segment will continue growing in the future.

However, Netflix is trading at overvalued levels, and I think the stock is likely to consolidate further. I don't think Netflix can experience a significant sell-off at this growth pace, but to see a momentum strengthen over the next 12 months with further upside, I would need more tailwinds to support this bullish stance.

On a PEG basis (TTM) 1.12, Netflix is about 2.7x overvalued compared to the industry average of 0.45. At an EV/Sales on a TTM basis at 12.70, the stock is almost 7x more expensive compared to peers. EV/EBITDA (TTM) at 41.95 represents nearly 4x overvaluation versus its competition, once again indicating that upside may be limited.

Forward PEG indicates that despite the bright outlook, the trend of overvaluation is not expected to change while the company trading at 1.422. For the stock to push further, it would need more investors to see it as an attractive entry point — and while I think this digital media behemoth is an incredible and high-quality business, from a simple fundamental analysis perspective, it is not trading at an attractive price.

The following valuation metrics also suggest that Netflix may be range-bound over the following year, with all of the metrics below indicating a massive overvaluation compared to the sector median:

Metrics Netflix Sector Median
Price/Sales [FWD] 11.56 1.28
Price/Book [FWD] 18.85 2.02
Price/Cash Flow [FWD] 55.81 7.75

Valuation is rich, and maintaining a Hold status instead of Sell is supported by strong profitability metrics. Netflix operates at a market-average gross profit margin of 48.49%. However, its EBITDA margin on a TTM basis is nearly 50% higher at 30.28% than its peers' 19.79%. Despite its levered FCF margin of 54%, nearly 5x the industry average, this indicates that, excluding all its obligations, Netflix sustains a healthy free cash flow to fund its business.

Netflix records a 19.30% return on total assets (TTM), while the average company in the industry struggles to return a positive figure at 2%. The digital behemoth has $9.07 billion in cash from operations (TTM) while its peers account for around $289 million, nearly 30x outperformance.

Netflix is simply an impressive business with barely any red flags. Its capital structure is healthy, with $8.39 billion in cash and total obligations of around $16.93 billion, making it a low-leverage business.

I think it is fair that this digital media company is trading at a premium, but I don't think valuations can stretch much further. That is why I believe it is likely to consolidate and trade in a range of about 10% from its current stock price of around $1,220. At a higher range of $1,300, it would put a forward P/E over 50, while trading at a lower bound of $1,100 would represent slightly above 41 — nearly 65% premium compared to an average of the S&P500 at 25. I think it is a rational range, considering promising guidance and better than market-average bottom-line growth. However, to see the stock price exceeding $1,300 in the near term, I think Netflix needs a strong tailwind.

Main Risks And Concerns

Netflix may be affected by multiple risks and concerns, which could weigh on the stock price over the near term.

I think that multiple contractions are one of the main near-term risks. While shares are trading at a significant premium compared to the broader market, a slight shift in sentiment could take the stock price down. Also, as it is trading at a high premium, it is priced to perfection with little room for error. The stock could decline 10%, and it would still be trading above 60% forward P/E compared to the average company of the benchmark.

Content production takes a huge part of the cost line for Netflix. It is a capital-intensive business, and to sustain rapid subscriber growth, it needs to offer high-quality content. If Netflix fails to succeed with a new series, it could impact engagement, and members could choose alternatives in a saturated digital media market.

Netflix operates in a highly competitive entertainment market with Amazon, YouTube, and other regional platforms competing for subscribers. Competition raises concerns around pricing power and pressures subscriber growth. I think that ad-supported tier is a great way to compete with other platforms, and it should continue playing a big part in Netflix's success.

On the upside, Netflix has a strong pipeline and a promising history of producing highly engaging content, such as Wednesday and Stranger Things. With continuations in the series and new content in progress, it is likely Netflix will continue its high-quality production.

I think that with the ad-supported tier approach, Netflix has great capabilities to expand in underpenetrated markets in Asia, Africa, and some parts of Europe. It suggests a long-term growth potential and supports my stance that Netflix's growth story continues, although valuations may need to come down slightly.

Conclusion: Netflix Is A Hold — At $1,000, I Would Consider Building A Position

Netflix is an exceptional, high-quality business that consistently produces high-profile content that keeps subscribers engaged. It is a healthy company, operating at high margins and growing its bottom line at a rapid pace, which helps sustain sky-high premiums. Although I would like the stock to drop below $1,000, I don't think it is likely, as the Netflix growth story continues.

However, at the current valuations, it is a Hold for me. I think the stock will trade in a range of about 10% from $1,100 to $1,300 over the next 12 months. If Netflix grew its bottom line more than 20% over the next 3 years, it would then be priced at market-average valuation, which suggests that the opportunity cost of investing in Netflix may be too high.

46 Wall Street analysts suggest a $1,350.32 price target over the next 12 months, indicating more than 10% upside. I think it is possible, considering how well Netflix is managed, but with the stock market rallying since April lows without a significant pullback and Netflix priced to perfection, I think the upside is limited.
